core_behavior: The system shall intercept outgoing API requests and, before forwarding them to the upstream API endpoint, check whether a valid cached response exists for the given request.
If a valid cache entry exists, the system shall return the cached response immediately without contacting the upstream API.
If no valid cache entry exists, the system shall forward the request to the upstream API, store the response in the cache, and return the response to the caller.
The cache shall operate as a read-through, write-on-miss layer sitting between the application and the upstream API.
inputs: Only GET and HEAD requests shall be cached by default. POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, and OPTIONS requests shall bypass the cache unless explicitly configured otherwise.
The full URL including scheme, host, path, and query string is used as a cache lookup input.

Headers that affect response content and are designated as cache-key contributors (e.g., Accept, Accept-Language, Authorization — configurable per endpoint) are used selectively as cache lookup inputs.
Request Body is included in cache key computation only for explicitly configured non-idempotent methods (e.g., POST used as a query endpoint).
TTL (Time-To-Live): Per-endpoint or global maximum age of a cache entry, expressed in seconds. Default: 300 seconds (5 minutes).
Cache-Control directives are honored from both upstream API responses (max-age, no-store, no-cache, private, s-maxage) and from incoming client requests (no-cache, no-store).
Maximum cache size: Configurable upper bound on total cache storage (entries or bytes). Default: 10,000 entries or 500 MB, whichever is reached first.
Cacheable status codes: Configurable list of HTTP status codes eligible for caching. Default: 200, 203, 204, 206, 300, 301, 404, 405, 410, 414, 501.
Excluded endpoints: A configurable list of URL patterns (glob or regex) that must never be cached.
The system shall respect the Vary response header from upstream APIs to differentiate cache entries by request header values.
outputs: On cache hit: the original upstream API response body.
On cache hit: all original upstream response headers, plus X-Cache: HIT, X-Cache-Age: <seconds since entry was cached>, and Age: <seconds> (RFC 7234 compliant).
On cache hit: HTTP status code identical to the cached upstream response.
On cache miss: the live upstream API response body, forwarded verbatim.
On cache miss: all original upstream response headers, plus X-Cache: MISS and X-Cache-Age: 0.
On cache miss: HTTP status code identical to the upstream response.
On cache bypass: the live upstream API response body, forwarded verbatim.
On cache bypass: all original upstream response headers, plus X-Cache: BYPASS (with reason: NO_STORE, PRIVATE, EXCLUDED_ENDPOINT, METHOD_NOT_CACHEABLE, etc.).
On cache bypass: the response is not written to the cache.
Cache Key: a deterministic, opaque string (e.g., SHA-256 hash) derived from: normalized URL + contributing request headers + HTTP method + (optionally) request body hash.
assumptions: The system uses an in-process LRU (Least Recently Used) cache by default, with optional configuration to use an external distributed cache (e.g., Redis, Memcached).
The storage backend is abstracted behind an interface to allow substitution.
URLs are normalized before key generation: query parameters are sorted alphabetically, trailing slashes are canonicalized, and percent-encoding is normalized to uppercase.
By default, requests bearing an Authorization header are [specification truncated].
